data.text/html:
Tiny Neural OS

 arXiv:2604.06425 View on GitHub

data.text/markdown:
Tiny Neural OS: Learning to Predict Terminal Screens

The core question: Can a neural network learn how a computer works just by watching the screen change? This notebook revisits the central idea from Neural Computers (arXiv:2604.06425). The paper proposes that a neural system can internalize computation, memory, and I/O by learning from interface traces alone — no explicit program state required. Rather than reimplement the full paper, we isolate one essential primitive:
Given the current terminal screen and an action, predict what the screen looks like next.
This simple task exposes the core tension:
  • Typing transitions are mechanical and local — the cursor moves, a character appears
  • Enter transitions are semantic — the model must know what the command does

What this notebook implements

  1. A toy terminal environment (10×40 character grid)
  2. A next-screen prediction task from screen-action traces
  3. A clean split between easy transitions (typing) and hard transitions (Enter)
  4. A CPU-trainable MLP baseline that predicts cell updates from local context
  5. A benchmark comparison against Transformer and GRU baselines

Why this matches the competition

The competition asks for a notebook that brings a paper's core idea to life through code, UI elements, and explanatory text — not a full reimplementation.
Paper's vision A learned neural runtime for interactive computing
This notebook A toy CLI that makes next-screen prediction legible
Our extension Benchmark framing: typing vs. Enter, paraphrase generalization
Standalone note: Everything runs here — simulator, dataset builder, feature encoder, training loop, and interactive analysis — all in notebook cells. External scripts are optional.

Part 1: Building a Toy Terminal

We need training data. Our terminal simulator:
  1. Maintains a screen buffer — a 10×40 grid of characters
  2. Processes actions — typing, backspace, Enter
  3. Produces screen transitions — (before, after) pairs for each action

The Command Library

Each command belongs to a family with multiple phrasings that produce the same output:
Family Phrasings Output
pwd pwd, echo $PWD, printenv PWD /home/researcher
whoami whoami, echo $USER, id -un researcher
This lets us test generalization: does the model understand command semantics, or just memorize character sequences?

data.text/html:
Data Structures

Structure Purpose
TerminalConfig Terminal dimensions (rows, columns)
Action A single user action (type char, Enter, backspace)
Episode A complete command sequence: type → Enter → output

Example Episode

data.text/markdown:
The Episode Generator

For each episode:
  1. Start with a blank screen: $ █
  2. Simulate typing each character of the command
  3. Simulate pressing Enter to execute
Each step produces one screen transition — our training data.

Part 2: Visualizing the Terminal

Step through an episode frame by frame. Watch for:
  • Yellow cells = what changed from the previous frame
  • Typing = 2 cells change (new char + cursor move)
  • Enter = 20–40 cells change (output + new prompt)

The Key Insight: Typing vs. Enter

Action Cells Changed What the Model Needs
Typing 2 Local rule: cursor → char, next cell → cursor
Enter 20–40 Semantic knowledge: what does this command output?
This is why we evaluate them separately — a model could hit 95% overall by acing typing while completely failing Enter.

data.text/markdown:
Part 3: Building the Training Dataset

The prediction task: For each cell, predict the character after the action.
  • Input (X): Current screen, cell position, action info
  • Output (y): Next character (class index 0–96)

Features per cell

Feature Dimensions Description
Local 3×3 patch 873 One-hot encoding of 9 neighbors
Position 2 Normalized (row, col)
Action type 4 One-hot: idle, type, backspace, enter
Command family 5 One-hot: pwd, whoami, date, ...
Typed character 97 One-hot: which char was typed
The last three are conditioning features — they tell the model what action is happening.

console.name: stderr
console.text: [W 260418 06:47:35 altair_transformer:79] Failed to convert data to arrow format, falling back to CSV: No module named 'pyarrow'

data.text/markdown:
Sampling Strategy

In a typical transition: ~395 cells stay the same, ~5 cells change. Training on all cells equally → the model learns to copy everything (98% accuracy, 0% on cells that matter). Our fix:
  • Keep all changed cells as positives
  • Sample a limited set of unchanged cells as negatives
  • This forces the model to learn real patterns, not just copying

console.name: stderr
console.text: [W 260418 06:47:36 altair_transformer:79] Failed to convert data to arrow format, falling back to CSV: No module named 'pyarrow'

data.text/markdown:
Part 4: The MLP Baseline Model

We use a simple Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP):
Layer Size Description
Input 981 Features (or fewer with less conditioning)
Hidden 128 ReLU activation
Output 97 Softmax over vocabulary
Loss: Cross-entropy, Loss = -log(P(correct_char)) Why MLP? It treats each cell independently, using only local context. This is a strong baseline because:
  1. Most screen changes are local
  2. The 3×3 patch usually suffices
  3. Fast to train on CPU
Weakness: Enter predictions require knowing command outputs — more than local context.

The Training Code

Here is the actual training code. We use scikit-learn's MLPClassifier:

Try It Yourself

Train an MLP with different settings to see how conditioning affects accuracy.

Visualizing the Asymmetry

Conditioning levels

These three settings let us test how much action information the model really needs.

Sample dataset statistics

# ==========================================================================
# TRAINING CODE
# ==========================================================================

from sklearn.neural_network import MLPClassifier

# 1. Generate training and test episodes
train_episodes = generate_episodes(n=40, seed=42)
test_episodes = generate_episodes(n=25, seed=999)

# 2. Build the training dataset
# - X: feature matrix (n_samples, 981 features)
# - y: labels (character indices 0-96)
X_train, y_train, stats = build_training_dataset(
    train_episodes,
    conditioning="full",  # use all features
    neg_ratio=8,            # 8 unchanged cells per changed cell
    seed=42,
)

print(f"Training samples: {len(X_train):,}")
print(f"Features per sample: {X_train.shape[1]}")

# 3. Create and train the MLP
model = MLPClassifier(
    hidden_layer_sizes=(128,),   # one hidden layer with 128 neurons
    max_iter=150,
    learning_rate_init=0.001,
    batch_size=256,
    random_state=42,
    early_stopping=True,
    validation_fraction=0.1,
)

# Train the model (this is where the learning happens)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

print(f"Converged in {model.n_iter_} iterations")

# 4. Evaluate on test episodes
metrics = evaluate_model(model, test_episodes, conditioning="full")

print(f"Overall accuracy: {100 * metrics['overall_acc']:.1f}%")
print(f"Typing accuracy:  {100 * metrics['typing_acc']:.1f}%")
print(f"Enter accuracy:   {100 * metrics['enter_acc']:.1f}%")
Configure training:

data.text/markdown:
Part 5: Benchmark Comparison

We compare three architectures:
Model Architecture Key Property
MLP Per-cell classifier Fast, local-only
Transformer Attention over screen Global dependencies
GRU Recurrent over screen Sequential processing

Four experimental settings

Setting Train/Test Conditioning
Standard / Family Same commands Command family only
Standard / Command Same commands Exact command text
Paraphrase / Family Train phrasing 1, test 2–3 Family only
Paraphrase / Command Train phrasing 1, test 2–3 Exact text
Paraphrase tests generalization: can the model handle echo $USER after only seeing whoami?

data.text/markdown:
Derived views: conditioning gain and paraphrase penalty

The grouped bar chart shows the raw scores, but two derived questions are especially useful:
  1. How much does exact command information help? This is the gain from Command over Family within the same train/test regime.
  2. How much does paraphrase hurt? This is the drop from Standard to Paraphrase under the same conditioning level.
These views make the benchmark easier to read because they turn four settings into two interpretable comparisons.

data.text/markdown: Full Results Table

data.text/html:
Understanding the Results

Several patterns emerge from the benchmark: 1. MLP is surprisingly strong. Despite being the simplest model, MLP achieves the highest overall accuracy. This is because most screen changes are local — a simple patch-based classifier captures them well. 2. Conditioning matters. All models improve dramatically when given exact command information ("Standard / Command" vs "Standard / Family"). This makes sense — knowing the exact typed characters makes prediction much easier. 3. Paraphrase is harder. When we train on "whoami" but test on "echo $USER", accuracy drops. This measures whether models learn command semantics or just memorize character patterns. 4. Enter is the hard case. For all models, Enter accuracy is lower than Typing accuracy in the generalization settings.

data.text/markdown: Part 6: Conclusions

data.text/html:
Key Findings

What this means for Neural Computers

The paper's vision — learning computation from screen traces — runs into a fundamental split:
  • Local patterns are learnable
  • Semantic understanding requires more (larger context, diverse pre-training, or explicit memory)

Reproducibility

experiments/toy_nc_cli/
├── scripts/           # smoke_test.py, train_transformer_baseline.py, train_gru_baseline.py
└── results/           # baseline_comparison.csv (benchmark data shown above)
